surahs.name: الفَاتِحَة
surahs.why: You recite this at least 17 times a day in prayer. Understanding it word-by-word transforms every salah.

verses.en: I begin in the name of Allah—calling on the Rahman, whose mercy in this life extends over every creature whether they recognize Him or not, and the Rahim, whose fullest mercy is reserved for those who turn to Him in the Akhirah.
words: بِ is a preposition (‘with/by’). Because a preposition precedes it, ism takes the genitive ending ‑i (‘bismi’). The whole phrase leans on an unspoken verb: ‘[I begin] with Allah’s name.’
س‑م‑و (s‑m‑w) — height; a ‘name’ raises and stands for a thing.
The name ‘Allah’ comes from al‑ilāh, ‘THE God’. Genitive ‑i here (‘l‑lāhi’) because it is ‘the name OF Allah’.
An attribute of Allah; genitive ‑i to match ‘Allah’ which it describes. The shape faʿlān = intensity, a mercy that is vast right now.
ر‑ح‑م (r‑ḥ‑m) — mercy, the tenderness of the womb (raḥim).
Attribute of Allah, genitive ‑i agreeing with ‘Allah’. faʿīl = a constant, enduring trait (paired with Raḥmān: vast mercy + lasting mercy).

verses.en: All praise truly belongs to Allah—the Rabb who owns, nurtures, and sustains al-alamin, everything that exists: this earth, the worlds beyond our sight, and every form of life across them.
words: Subject of the sentence, so it takes the nominative ending ‑u (‘al‑ḥamdu’). The ‘al‑’ makes it total: ALL praise of every kind.
لِ is the preposition of ownership/right (‘to, for, belonging to’); it puts Allah in the genitive ‑i. Sense: the praise is rightfully His.

The ending ‑i (‘rabbi’) is the GENITIVE case: it ties rabb to what follows — ‘Lord OF the worlds’ — and matches ‘Allah’. (Note: رَبِّي ‘rabbī’, with an extra ‑ī, would instead mean ‘MY Lord’ — that final ‑ī is the possessive ‘my’.)
ر‑ب‑ب (r‑b‑b) — to nurture, sustain, own, master.
Sound masculine plural. The ‑īna ending does double duty: it marks the plural AND the genitive (‘Lord OF the worlds’). ‘ʿālam’ is literally ‘that by which the Creator is known’.

verses.en: —the Rahman whose compassion reaches even those who deny Him in this dunya, and the Rahim whose deepest mercy awaits those who live by iman in the Akhirah.
words: Attribute of Allah/‘the Lord’; genitive ‑i to agree with it. faʿlān = vast, present mercy.
Attribute, genitive ‑i. faʿīl = a lasting, settled quality.

verses.en: Sovereign of the Day of Reckoning—when every hidden deed, every word spoken in private, every kindness forgotten, and every account left unsettled in this life will be weighed with perfect justice.
words: Active participle (‘the one who owns’). Genitive ‑i: it both describes ‘Allah’ and is in construct — ‘Master OF the Day’.
Genitive ‑i: in construct ‘Day OF Judgment’, and governed by ‘Master of’.
ي‑و‑م (y‑w‑m) — day, a span of time.
Genitive ‑i (construct ‘Day OF Recompense’). The same word dīn elsewhere means ‘religion/way’; context fixes the meaning as ‘requital’.
د‑ي‑ن (d‑y‑n) — to owe and repay, to judge.

verses.en: You alone we worship with full devotion—we do not give our ultimate loyalty to wealth, status, approval, or power. You alone we ask for help—knowing that no system, person, or plan can replace reliance on You.
words: ‘iyyā’ is a support that carries the object pronoun ‑ka (‘You’). Fronting it before the verb = exclusivity: ‘You ALONE we worship’.
Present‑tense verb. The na‑ prefix = ‘we’; the final ‑u marks it as an ongoing, affirmed action.
wa = ‘and’; the object ‘You’ is again placed first to keep the meaning ‘You and no one else’.
Present‑tense ‘we’ verb. The added ‑st‑ (form X) means ‘to seek/ask for’, so the verb becomes ‘we SEEK help’.

verses.en: Guide us to the Sirat al-Mustaqim—the way of living that aligns with Your pleasure, not the path of confusion offered by a world that keeps changing what it calls truth.
words: A request form (‘guide!’) addressed to Allah, softened by humility into a prayer. ‑nā attached = ‘us’ (the object).
ه‑د‑ي (h‑d‑y) — to guide, to show the way.
Object of ‘guide us [to]’, so it takes the accusative ending ‑a (‘aṣ‑ṣirāṭa’).
Adjective describing ‘path’, so it shares its accusative ‑a. A form‑X participle: ‘that which stands perfectly straight’.
ق‑و‑م (q‑w‑m) — to stand, to be upright.

verses.ar: صِرَٰطَ ٱلَّذِينَ أَنۡعَمۡتَ عَلَيۡهِمۡ غَيۡرِ ٱلۡمَغۡضُوبِ عَلَيۡهِمۡ وَلَا ٱلضَّآلِّينَ
verses.en: The path of those You favoured with guidance—not the path of those who knew the truth and chose resentment, nor of those who lost their way through neglect, distraction, or following every passing voice.
words: Stands in apposition to the earlier ‘path’ (accusative ‑a), and is in construct: ‘path OF those…’.
Relative pronoun, masculine plural (‘those who…’), in the genitive slot (‘path OF those who…’).
Past‑tense verb. The ending ‑ta = ‘You’ (addressing Allah). Form IV (afʿala) = to confer/bestow a favor.
Means ‘other than’; genitive ‑i agreeing with ‘those’. It marks off who is NOT on the straight path.
Passive participle (‘the ones wrathed‑upon’), genitive ‑i. With ‘ʿalayhim’ it reads ‘those upon whom is anger’.
wa ‘and’ + lā ‘not’. The repeated negation shows these are two separate groups to avoid.
Active participle, sound masculine plural; the ‑īna ending marks plural + genitive. ‘the ones straying from the way’.
ض‑ل‑ل (ḍ‑l‑l) — to lose the way, to err.

surahs.name: الإِخْلَاص
surahs.why: Equal to a third of the Quran in reward, and built entirely from top-frequency words: قل، هو، الله، لم، يكن، له.

verses.en: Say to them: He is Allah—the One. Not one among many, not one part of a pair, but absolutely singular in His essence, deserving worship with no partner whatsoever.
words: Command verb (imperative), addressed to a single male — "you, say!" It comes from the verb qāla ("to say"). In the command form the middle weak letter (wāw) drops out, leaving a short, sharp qul that ends in a silent (sukūn) lām — exactly how a direct order to one person is formed.

Detached subject pronoun, third-person masculine singular ("He"). Arabic has no separate word for "is," so "He" + "Allah" together already mean "He is Allah." Its fixed final -a (huwa) is part of the word itself, not a case ending, since pronouns do not change for case.
Proper name (the divine name). The al- at the front is part of the name itself. The final -u (…hu) is the nominative (raf') ending: it marks Allah as the predicate identifying "He," i.e.…
— (proper name; traditionally linked to the idea of the one worthy of worship, but treated as a name, not a derived word)

Noun/adjective acting as a second predicate — "[He is] One." The ending -un (aḥadun) is the nominative case combined with tanwīn (the doubled "n" sound) that marks it as indefinite — "One," not "the one" — underscoring His absolute, unshared oneness.…

verses.en: Allah is al-Samad—the One every created thing depends upon for existence, while He depends on nothing; the eternal refuge when every system, relationship, and plan fails.
words: the (definite article, fused into the name) God (the divine Name)

This is the subject of the sentence, and the verse is a pure equational sentence with no verb ('Allah [is] the Self-Sufficient'). The final -u ending is the nominative case, which is the marker Arabic puts on the subject — the one the statement is about.…
Linguistically connected to ʾ-l-h (relating to worship/divinity), but as the unique proper Name of God it is treated as having no ordinary derivable root — —
the the Self-Sufficient One on whom all depend

This word is the predicate — it tells us what Allah is. Its final -u ending is the nominative case, matching the subject 'Allah' (both subject and predicate of an equational sentence take this ending).…
ص-م-د (ṣ-m-d) — to turn to / resort to (someone in authority); hence the One sought out by all, who is Himself in need of none

verses.en: He has never begotten a child, nor was He born—free from the cycle of birth and death that defines everything in this dunya.
words: A particle of negation (called a "jussive" particle) that governs the verb after it: it changes a present-tense verb into a negated past meaning and clips its ending to a bare, vowel-less form. So although the next word looks present-tense, "lam" makes it mean "He did not / has never."

A present-tense verb, 3rd-person masculine singular: the "ya-" prefix marks "he." The root is و-ل-د, but in the active form the first root-letter waw drops out (a regular feature of waw-initial verbs), leaving just "-lid." Because the particle "lam" governs it, the verb is in the clipped (jussive) form —…
و-ل-د (w-l-d) — to give birth, beget, offspring

Two pieces joined: the prefix "wa-" means "and," and "lam" is again the negation particle that puts the following verb into the negated-past, clipped (jussive) form. Together they add a parallel second negation to the verse.
he (is) — passive root-letter "w" of w-l-d (lengthened to ū) born / begotten

A present-tense verb in the PASSIVE voice, 3rd-person masculine singular — "he is born / begotten" (something done TO him), not "he gives birth." The passive is shown by the vowel pattern: a "u" on the "yu-" prefix and a fatha ("a") on the last stem-letter (yūlad rather than the active yalid).…
و-ل-د (w-l-d) — to give birth, beget, offspring

verses.en: And there is nothing—no idol, no ideology, no celebrity, no created power—that stands as His equal or counterpart in any way.
words: Two pieces joined. wa- is the connector "and." lam is a negative particle that does two things at once: it flips a present-tense verb into a denied past ("did not / has never"), and it is jussive (jazm), meaning it strips the ending off the following verb down to a bare sukūn (no vowel).…
— (particles: wa = "and," lam = negation; no triliteral root)
he / it (3rd-person masculine prefix) be / exist (from kāna)

An imperfect (present-tense) verb, 3rd-person masculine singular, "he/it is," built from the prefix ya- ("he/it") on the stem of kāna. Two things shape its form: (1) lam before it makes it mean a denied past — "has not been";…
ك-و-ن (k-w-n) — being, existing, coming to be

A preposition plus an attached pronoun. The preposition is li- ("to / for / belonging to"); when an attached pronoun follows, li- regularly shifts its vowel to la- before the pronoun -hu, giving the surface form lahu (recited with a slight lengthening, lahū). -hu is the attached pronoun "Him," referring to Allah.…
equal / comparable / a match indefinite accusative ending ("a / an," object-case)

A noun, "an equal." The ending is -an (written as tanwīn fatḥ, the double-fatḥa on the final alif), which marks two things at once: the doubled-n sound (tanwīn) means it is indefinite — "an equal," not "the equal"; and the -a vowel is the accusative case (manṣūb), which flags it as the predicate (khabar) of kāna.…
ك-ف-ء (k-f-ʾ) — being equal, matching, being a counterpart
one / anyone indefinite nominative ending ("a / an," subject-case)

A noun, "anyone." The ending is -un (tanwīn ḍamm, the double-ḍamma). The doubled-n (tanwīn) marks it as indefinite — "any one"; the -u vowel is the nominative case (marfūʿ), which identifies it as the subject (ism) of yakun ("there has [never] been").…
ء-ح-د (ʾ-ḥ-d) — oneness, unity, a single one

surahs.name: العَصْر
surahs.why: Three verses containing the Quran's core message — and its highest-frequency structures: إنّ، الذين، آمنوا، عملوا، الحق.

verses.en: By time itself—every hour scrolling, every season passing, every deadline you feel slipping away—Allah swears an oath that what follows is among the most urgent truths you will ever hear.
words: by (an oath: 'I swear by…') the time / the passing age (late afternoon)

Here wa- is the particle of oath ('By…!'), not the ordinary 'and.' Grammatically it works as a governing particle (harf jarr) — the same class as prepositions — and any noun it governs is put in the genitive (majrūr) case.…
ع-ص-ر (ʿ-ṣ-r) — to press/squeeze out (as juice is pressed); from it ʿaṣr, the portion of the day being 'pressed' to its end, hence the late afternoon and, more broadly, time and the passing age.

verses.en: Humanity as a whole is heading toward loss—squandering the one resource that never returns: the moments of a life that could have been lived with purpose.
words: A particle of emphasis and confirmation. Grammatically it governs the noun after it, forcing that noun ('the human being') into the accusative case — which is exactly why the next word ends in -a (al-insāna). It does not translate as a word so much as a tone of absolute certainty laid over the sentence.
the human being, mankind accusative ending

Definite noun ('al-' = 'the'), here used to mean the whole human race. It is the topic the sentence makes a statement about (the ism of 'inna'). Normally such a subject would take the nominative, but the particle 'inna' before it overrides that and puts its noun into the accusative —…
أ-ن-س (ʾ-n-s) — to be sociable, familiar, human

Two pieces joined together. The prefix 'la-' is the 'lām of emphasis' that pairs with 'inna' from word 1 to double down on the certainty ('inna... la...' = 'most surely'). 'fī' is the preposition 'in', and it puts the following noun into the genitive case, which is why 'loss' next ends in -in.…

Indefinite noun. The ending -in does two jobs at once: the -i part is the genitive case (required because the preposition 'fī' came before it, 'in [a state of] loss'), and the final -n sound (tanwīn) marks it as indefinite ('a loss', not 'the loss'). Leaving it indefinite hints at a loss so vast it is left undefined.
خ-س-ر (kh-s-r) — to lose, suffer loss, be ruined

verses.ar: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُواْ وَعَمِلُواْ ٱلصَّٰلِحَٰتِ وَتَوَاصَوۡاْ بِٱلۡحَقِّ وَتَوَاصَوۡاْ بِٱلصَّبۡرِ
verses.en: Except those who carry true iman—not mere opinion but trust that reshapes how they live; who do righteous deeds that help real people; who keep urging one another toward haqq, what is objectively true in a world drowning in spin; and who keep urging one another to sabr—the steadfast endurance that keeps you upright when progress is slow and pain is real.
words: This is an indeclinable exception particle (it never changes its ending). It does not carry case itself; instead it introduces the excepted group that follows, telling you 'everyone is in loss — apart from these.'
the (relative marker; its lām is doubled) those who (masc. plural)

A masculine plural relative pronoun, 'those who.' Its form is fixed (it does not change for case here); the -īna shape marks it as plural and masculine. The shadda over the lām in الَّذِيۡنَ means the lām is doubled, so the two parts reconstruct as al-l + dhīna = alladhīna.…

A past-tense (form IV) verb. Form IV (the lengthened a- at the start, originally 'a-) here carries the sense 'to come to believe, to have faith.' The -ū ending means the subject is 'they' (a group of males, or a mixed group). So: 'they believed.'

A simple joining particle, 'and.' It has no case ending of its own; it merely couples what follows to what came before, here adding a second quality to the believers. (Note: in this source the transliteration 'waʿamilū' bundles this 'and' together with the following verb 'they did.')
A past-tense (form I) verb, 'they did/worked.' The -ū ending marks the subject as 'they' (masc. plural). It governs the next word as its object: 'they did [the righteous deeds].'
the righteous / good (deeds) sound feminine plural ending

A sound feminine plural noun/adjective, 'the righteous deeds,' acting as the object of 'they did.' Its ending is -āti (with kasra on the tā'). For sound feminine plurals this same -āti ending serves BOTH the accusative and the genitive — the ending alone does not distinguish the two cases.…
ص-ل-ح (ṣ-l-ḥ) — to be sound, upright, good
and mutually advised / urged one another they (masc. plural)

'And' + a past-tense (form VI) verb. Form VI (the ta- prefix with the lengthened -ā-) signals a reciprocal action done back and forth between people: 'they advised one another.' The plural 'they' ending (here written -aw) marks the subject as a group.…
with / to / regarding the truth / what is right

The particle bi- ('with/to') joined to 'the truth.' Because bi- is a preposition, the noun after it takes the genitive case, shown by the final -i (ḥaqqi). It links back to the verb 'urged one another,' answering 'urged each other to/with what?' — namely, the truth.
ح-ق-ق (ḥ-q-q) — truth, right, what is real and due
and mutually advised / urged one another they (masc. plural) to / with the patience / steadfast endurance

'And' + the same reciprocal form VI verb 'they urged one another' (ta- prefix, lengthened -ā-, plural 'they' ending), followed by bi- ('to/with') plus 'the patience.' After the preposition bi-, the noun takes the genitive case, marked by the final -i (ṣabri).…
ص-ب-ر (ṣ-b-r) — to be patient, endure, persevere

surahs.name: الكَوْثَر
surahs.why: The shortest surah — and you already know فَصَلِّ and لِرَبِّكَ from your word families.

verses.en: We have given you, O Prophet, al-Kawthar—abundant good in this life and the next: honour, a lasting legacy, intercession, and a river in Paradise that those who mocked you will never reach.
words: Inna is an emphasising particle that opens the sentence and makes its claim sound certain and weighty ('it is truly the case that...'). The attached ending -nā is the pronoun 'we', referring to Allah in the royal/majestic plural (a plural of greatness, not of number).…
gave / granted (Form IV verb stem) we (the doers) you (singular, addressed to the Prophet)

This is a past-tense (perfect) verb: the action is presented as already done and complete ('have granted'). It is built on Form IV (the أَفۡعَلَ pattern), whose defining mark is the prefixed hamza (أَ) added to the root — a pattern that typically carries a causative 'to make someone have / to give' sense.…

The al- prefix is the definite article 'the', marking this as a specific, known thing — 'the Abundance'. The word itself is an intensive noun (the فَوۡعَل / kawthar pattern) signalling not just 'much' but 'exceedingly much, overflowing'.…

verses.en: So establish salah for your Lord alone, and offer sacrifice—public worship and tangible gratitude that show who truly owns your time and loyalty.
words: so / therefore (connecting this to what came before) pray! (perform the ritual prayer)
'fa-' is a connector meaning 'so/therefore,' linking the command to the gift of the previous verse. 'ṣalli' is a direct command (imperative) addressed to a single male —…
ص-ل-و (ṣ-l-w) — to pray, to perform worship; connection/supplication to Allah
to / for (the sake of) Lord, Master, Sustainer your (one male)

'li-' is a prefix meaning 'to/for,' marking the one for whose sake the prayer is offered. Because of this 'li-', the noun 'rabb' takes the genitive '-i' ending (rabbi) — the case Arabic uses after such prefixes and to show linkage/possession. The attached 'ka' on the end means 'your,' addressed to a single male;…
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, sustain, master; lordship and care

'wa-' simply means 'and,' joining this command to the command to pray. 'inḥar' is a direct command (imperative) to a single male — 'you, sacrifice!' When the imperative is formed from this Form I verb, the first root letter is left with no vowel of its own, so the stem would begin with the cluster 'n-ḥ' —…
ن-ح-ر (n-ḥ-r) — to slaughter (esp. a camel, by piercing the upper chest/neck); ritual sacrifice

verses.en: It is your enemy who is truly cut off—severed from lasting good, from remembrance, and from the reward he assumed his mockery could erase.
words: This is an emphasizing particle (ḥarf tawkīd) that introduces a nominal sentence and grammatically governs the noun after it, putting that noun into the accusative case — which is why the next word ends in -a. It carries no tense or person of its own; its whole job is to stress the truth of the sentence.
the one who hates / detractor you (your)

This is an active participle (shāniʾ, 'the hater / one who hates') — a noun-like form naming the doer of the action — joined to the attached pronoun -ka, 'your'. It is the subject (ism) governed by inna, so it carries the accusative -a ending (shāniʾa-) demanded by inna;…
ش-ن-أ (sh-n-ʾ) — to hate, to feel malice/aversion
A detached (independent) personal pronoun, third-person masculine singular, 'he'.…
the cut off / severed (without lineage or trace)

This is the definite article al- ('the') attached to abtar, an adjective on the afʿal pattern. Here afʿal is the defect/physical-attribute type (ṣifah mushabbahah, like aḥmar 'red' or aʿraj 'lame') — it names a permanent quality, 'cut off', and is NOT the comparative/superlative (elative) sense;…
ب-ت-ر (b-t-r) — to cut off, sever, dock (leave without continuation)

surahs.name: النَّاس
surahs.why: The Quran's final surah, recited daily for protection — six short verses around one root: النَّاس.

verses.en: Say: I seek refuge in the Rabb of humanity—the One who owns and cares for every human being, regardless of tribe, passport, or status.
words: A command (imperative) verb addressed to a single male: "you, say!" The base verb is qāla / yaqūlu ("to say"). In the command form the weak middle letter drops out, leaving the short, clipped qul ending in a silent consonant — the bare, urgent form of an order.

A present-tense verb in the first person singular: "I seek refuge" (right now / continually). The a- prefix marks the doer as "I." The final -u ending is the ordinary present-tense (indicative) marker, signalling a plain, ongoing statement of fact rather than a command or a wish.
ع-و-ذ (ʿ-w-dh) — to seek refuge, to take shelter / protection

A particle bi- ("in, with, by") attached to the front of the noun rabb. The bi- here conveys taking hold of Allah for protection ("I take refuge IN the Lord"). Any noun governed by bi- must take the genitive case, which is why rabb ends in -i (rabbi);…
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, sustain, master, bring up

The definite article al- ("the") prefixed to the noun nās ("people"). Because nās begins with a "solar" letter (n), the l of the article is not pronounced; instead the noun's own initial n is doubled, so the pair is read an-nās (the doubling is shown by the shadda on the noun).…

أ-ن-س (ʼ-n-s) — humankind, people; human sociability / familiarity (nās is a contraction of unās, with the hamza dropped in common usage); some lexicographers alternatively relate it to ن-و-س, n-w-s, "to sway, move about"

verses.en: The Sovereign of humanity—whose authority no government, corporation, or algorithm can override.
words: A noun standing in apposition to 'Rabbi' (Lord) from the previous verse, so it shares the same role. The final -i ending is genitive (the majrūr case): here it does two jobs at once —…
م-ل-ك (m-l-k) — ownership, dominion, kingship, sovereign authority
the (definite article 'al-', its 'l' assimilated into the following 'n') people / mankind (the stem 'nās')

A definite noun: the prefix 'al-' is 'the', but its 'l' is not pronounced — it merges into the doubled 'n' of 'nās' (so 'an-nās', not 'al-nās'), 'n' being a sun letter. The final -i ending is genitive, marking it as the second part of the possessive construct (the muḍāf ilayhi): 'King OF mankind'.

أ-ن-س (a-n-s) — sociability, companionship, humanity; the mainstream derivation, where 'al-nās' is held to be a contraction of 'al-unās' (people). (A minority instead trace it to ن-و-س, n-w-s, 'to sway'.)

verses.en: The One humanity truly worships, whether they admit it or not—since whatever people give their ultimate fear, love, and obedience to becomes, in practice, what they serve.
words: god / deity (the one worthy of worship) of (genitive ending linking to the next word)

A singular masculine noun in the genitive case, functioning as the possessed term (muḍāf) of a possessive construction (iḍāfa) — it continues the chain begun in the previous verses, standing in apposition to 'Lord' and 'King'.…
أ-ل-ه (ʾ-l-h) — worship, divinity; the quality of being the one deserving of worship

A definite collective noun ('the people / mankind') in the genitive case, functioning as the possessor (muḍāf ilayhi) of the preceding word. The prefix al- is 'the'; its 'l' assimilates into the following 'n' (a sun letter), which is why it is pronounced an-nās and written with a doubled n (shadda).…
ن-و-س (n-w-s) — to move/stir about; the sense behind nās, 'humankind, people' (the stronger majority view derives it from أ-ن-س, ʾ-n-s, relating to companionship and being human)

verses.en: From the evil of the whisperer who withdraws—the insidious voice that plants doubt, vanity, rage, or despair and then retreats, leaving you to think the thought was entirely your own.
words: This is a non-inflecting preposition (it never changes its ending). It governs the noun after it and forces that noun into the genitive case — which is why the next word 'sharr' takes an -i ending (sharri). Think of it as 'from' pulling whatever follows into a 'from-the-X' relationship.
The final -i ending is genitive, and it is doing two jobs at once. First, it is genitive because the preposition 'min' before it requires that case ('from the evil').…

The prefix 'al-' is 'the', and its 'l' is pronounced clearly here: waw (و) is a 'lunar' letter, so there is no assimilation — you say 'al-waswās' with an audible l. The word is built from the four-consonant (quadriliteral) root w-s-w-s, which is itself a reduplication (the pair w-s repeated).…
و-س-و-س (w-s-w-s) — to whisper, to insinuate faint promptings
the one who repeatedly withdraws/slinks away genitive ending
The prefix 'al-' is 'the', and its 'l' is pronounced clearly: khāʾ (خ) is a 'lunar' letter, so there is no assimilation — you say 'al-khannās' with an audible l.…
خ-ن-س (kh-n-s) — to draw back, recede, slink away furtively

verses.en: Who whispers into the hearts of people—among jinn and among humans—through suggestion, propaganda, envy, and the quiet narratives that reshape conscience without announcement.
words: This is a masculine singular relative pronoun, used here for a male/singular referent (the whisperer). It is a single fused, indivisible word — not a noun carrying a detachable "al-" article. The doubled l (shown by the shadda on لّ) belongs to the pronoun itself.…
he / it (present-tense "he does" marker) whisper, insinuate

This is a present/ongoing-tense verb, third-person masculine singular: "he whispers / keeps whispering." The yu- prefix marks "he," and the final -u ending shows the verb is in its base (indicative) state, signalling a real, habitual, continuing action — this is what the whisperer constantly does.…
و-س-و-س (w-s-w-s) — whispering, faint insinuating suggestion; a quadriliteral root formed by reduplicating the unit و-س

An indeclinable preposition: its form never changes. Grammatically it puts the noun that follows it ("breasts") into the genitive case, which is why that next word carries an -i ending. It conveys containment — the thoughts are cast within the inner self.
chests / breasts (plural) of (genitive ending, linking to the next word)

This is the plural of ṣadr ("chest"). It carries a final -i ending for two reasons working together: it follows the preposition "in" (fī), and it is the first part of a possessive link ("chests OF…"). That -i tells you it is genitive and that the next word completes the phrase — "the chests of mankind."
ص-د-ر (ṣ-d-r) — chest, breast; the inner front of a person
the (definite article; its "l" sound merges into the following n) people / humankind (with genitive -i ending)

A definite collective noun, "the people / mankind." The al- ("the") is written but its "l" sound assimilates into the following n, so it is pronounced an-nās. The final -i ending is genitive: it completes the possessive phrase begun by the previous word, giving "the chests OF mankind."

أ-ن-س (ʾ-n-s) — the standard root, from أَنِسَ (to be sociable/intimate); the initial hamza is dropped in an-nās. (A minority view derives it from ن-و-س, n-w-s, "to sway/move," but أ-ن-س is the dominant classical view.)

verses.en: From among jinn and mankind—reminding you that harm is not only supernatural; people, too, can whisper corruption into a community, a family, or a soul.
words: A preposition (a small linking word). It is normally just 'min', but here the silent ending is voiced as 'mina' (with a final -a) to ease pronunciation before the following word, which begins with the joining-hamza of 'al-'.…

A noun made definite by the prefix 'al-' ('the'). The final -i ending is the genitive case, triggered by the preposition 'min' before it ('from the jinn'). The -ah/-at ending marks it as a collective noun naming the whole category rather than one individual.…
ج-ن-ن (j-n-n) — to cover, conceal, hide (jinn are so named because they are hidden from sight)

Built from 'wa-' ('and') joined to the definite noun 'al-nās' ('the people'). Because it is linked by 'and' to the earlier genitive 'al-jinnah', it shares the same genitive case, shown by the final -i ending ('...and mankind'). In speech the 'l' of 'al-' assimilates into the 'n', so it sounds like 'an-nās'.
ن-و-س / أ-ن-س (n-w-s / ʾ-n-s) — humankind, people; relating to human sociability and movement

surahs.name: الفَلَق
surahs.why: One of the two protection surahs you recite morning and evening.

verses.en: Say: I seek refuge in the Rabb of the breaking dawn—the One who brings light after the deepest darkness, when fear feels loudest and the night feels endless.
words: A command verb (imperative) addressed to a single male. The root is q-w-l ('to say'), whose middle weak letter (و) drops in the command, leaving the short form qul. There is no extra suffix: the bare form with its final silent l is itself the order 'you (one person), say'.
I (the doer) seek refuge / take shelter

A present-tense verb from the root ʿ-w-dh ('to seek protection'). The a- prefix marks the doer as 'I', so it means 'I seek refuge' (right now / habitually). The final -u ending is the normal present-tense indicative vowel, showing a plain statement of fact ('I do seek refuge'), not a wish or command.
ع-و-ذ (ʿ-w-dh) — to seek refuge, to take shelter
in / with (taking hold of) Lord, Master and Sustainer

Two pieces joined: the attached preposition bi- ('in / with / by means of') plus the noun rabb ('Lord'). Two things act on rabb at once. First, sitting after the preposition bi- forces it into the genitive case, shown by the final -i (rabbi).…
the daybreak, the cleaving-open of dawn

The definite article al- ('the') joined to the noun falaq ('daybreak'). It is the second term of the possessive pairing 'Lord OF the daybreak', so it takes the genitive case, shown by the final -i ending (falaqi). The word is a noun built on the root meaning 'to split', picturing dawn as light splitting the dark.
ف-ل-ق (f-l-q) — to split, cleave, break open

verses.en: From the evil of what He created—recognizing that creation itself is not evil, but that harm can emerge from what He made when misused or when its darker potentials are unleashed.
words: This is an indeclinable preposition (it never changes form). It governs the following noun and puts it in the genitive case — which is why the next word, sharr, ends in -i (sharri). It expresses "starting-point" or source: the evil one is fleeing away from.

A noun in the genitive case. It is genitive for one reason only: it follows the preposition min ("from"), which always puts the word after it in the genitive. Sharr is also the first term (the possessed word, mudaf) of a possessive phrase — "the evil OF what He created" —…
ش-ر-ر (sh-r-r) — to be evil / wicked / harmful

An indeclinable relative pronoun (its form never changes). Grammatically it is the second term of the possessive phrase begun by sharr — the possessor — so it carries the sense "of": "the evil of [mā] = the evil of that which…" It then needs a verb to define it, supplied by the next word.

A past-tense verb, third-person masculine singular: the built-in subject is "he" (here, Allah). The final -a is the standard past-tense ending for "he did" — no extra subject word is needed. The phrase functions as the relative clause defining mā: "what He created."
خ-ل-ق (kh-l-q) — to create / originate / bring into being

verses.en: And from the evil of darkness when it settles—literal night and the inner night of despair, rumination, and the thoughts that grow when you are alone with your screen or your fears.
words: Two pieces joined together. The wa- is a connector meaning 'and', tying this verse to the request for refuge in the verse before. min is a preposition meaning 'from', marking the source or origin of the evil. A preposition like min puts the noun after it into the genitive case, which is why the next word ends in -i.

A noun meaning 'evil'. Its final -i ending is genitive for two reasons at once: it follows the preposition min ('from the evil…'), and it is the first term (the possessed word) of a possessive pairing ('the evil OF darkness') — so it leans on the next word to complete the sense.

An active participle — literally 'one that grows dark' — used here as a noun for the darkening night. It is the second term of the possessive pairing 'the evil OF darkness' (the possessor word), and that role is exactly what puts it in the genitive case — shown by the -i in its ending.…
غ-س-ق (gh-s-q) — to grow dark, darkness pouring in

A time word meaning 'when', normally pointing to something expected to happen. It introduces the following verb and pins the danger to a specific moment — the night is feared precisely 'when' it does what comes next. It has no case ending of its own.

A past-tense verb, 'he/it spread/settled in', with a built-in 'he/it' subject referring back to the darkening night. The final -a is the normal past-tense ending for this form. Though past in form, after idhā ('when') it reads as a recurring action: 'whenever it spreads'.
و-ق-ب (w-q-b) — to set in, sink in, overspread

verses.en: And from the evil of those who blow on knots—those who secretly work harm through envy, manipulation, or hidden schemes meant to undo your peace.
words: Two joined particles. The wa- prefix is a connector meaning 'and', tying this phrase to the earlier 'I seek refuge ... from ...'. 'min' is a preposition meaning 'from', and it forces the noun after it (sharri) into the genitive case, which is why that next word ends in -i.…

A noun. The final -i ending is genitive for two reasons at once: it comes right after the preposition 'min' ('from the evil...'), and it begins a possessive chain (iḍāfa) with the following word, giving the sense 'the evil OF the blowers'. The doubled 'r' (shadda) is part of the stem, not a grammatical ending.
the women who blow much/repeatedly (of) — genitive ending

A definite noun: 'al-' means 'the'. The stem belongs to the intensive faʿʿāl family (doubled middle letter), which signals doing something a lot or habitually. Its feminine singular base is naffātha (pattern faʿʿāla, ending in -a) = 'a woman who blows much';…
ن-ف-ث (n-f-th) — to blow, to spit/breathe out softly

A preposition meaning 'in' or 'into'. It governs the next word, putting 'the knots' into the genitive case (the -i ending you see there). A preposition has no tense or person; it simply links the action of blowing to its object.

A definite plural noun: 'al-' means 'the', and 'ʿuqad' is the plural of ʿuqda ('a knot'), so it means 'the knots'. The final -i ending is genitive because the word is the object of the preposition 'fī' ('in/upon the knots').…

verses.en: And from the evil of an envier when he envies—when someone resents your blessings, your stability, or your faith and wishes them gone.
words: Written as one word in the text (wamin) but built from two pieces. "wa-" is the connector "and," tying this verse to the earlier ones. "min" is a preposition meaning "from"; here it marks the source of evil one takes refuge against.…

A noun in the genitive case, shown by its final "-i." The "-i" ending appears for two reasons at once: it follows the preposition "min" ("from"), and it is the first half of a possessive pair ("the evil OF an envier"), linking it to the following word. So "sharri ḥāsidin" reads "the evil of an envier."

An active participle (the "-ā-" after the first root letter is the doer pattern, like "-er" in English: one who envies = an envier). Its final "-in" is genitive plus nunation (the "n" sound marks it as indefinite, "an" envier, not "the" envier).…

A time particle meaning "when / at the time that." It does not change its form (it has no case ending). It introduces the following verb and sets the condition: protection is sought from the envier precisely "when" the next action happens.

A past-tense verb, "he envied," with the bare "-a" ending that marks the basic third-person masculine singular ("he"). After the time-word "idhā" ("when"), this past-tense form carries a general, timeless sense — "whenever he envies" — rather than a single completed event.…

surahs.name: القَدْر
surahs.why: The night worth more than a thousand months — recited constantly in Ramadan.

verses.en: We sent it—the Qur'an—down on the Night of Decree, Laylat al-Qadr—
words: indeed / truly (strong emphasis) we (attached pronoun, the divine 'We')

'Inna' is an emphasis-particle placed at the head of a sentence to assert it firmly; render it as 'indeed' or 'most certainly'. The attached '-nā' is the pronoun 'we', referring to Allah in the majestic plural — not a literal numerical plural but a marker of supreme greatness.…
— (emphasis particle + attached pronoun; no triliteral root)
sent down / caused to descend (Form IV) we (subject: Allah, majestic plural) it / him (object: the Qur'an)

A past-tense verb. The pattern 'an-' (Form IV, from the root n-z-l 'to come down') is causative: it turns 'to descend' into 'to send/bring down'. The '-nā' ending marks the doer as 'we' (Allah, in the plural of majesty) and shows the action is completed (past). The final '-hu' is the object 'it', i.e.…

A preposition meaning 'in' or 'during'. It governs the noun that follows and puts it in the genitive case — which is why the next word 'laylat' takes its genitive ending. It frames the timing: the sending-down happened within this night.

A feminine noun, 'night'. Its final '-i' (genitive) is governed by the preceding preposition 'fī' ('in') — that alone sets the case. This word is also the first term (mudaf) of a possessive construction ('the Night OF the Power');…
the (definite article) power / decree / measuring-out / high worth

'al-' is the definite article 'the'. The noun 'qadr' carries a final '-i' (genitive) because it is the second term (mudaf ilayh) of the possessive construction begun by 'laylat' — it is the thing possessed ('the Night OF the Power'). The '-i' ending here marks that 'of' relationship.…
ق-د-ر (q-d-r) — to measure, decree, apportion; power, capability, worth

verses.en: And what will explain to you what the Night of Decree is?
words: Two pieces joined: the prefix wa- is the connector "and," linking this verse to the one before. The word mā here is an interrogative, "what," used not for real inquiry but rhetorically to stress the greatness of what follows. It has no case ending of its own; it stands as the subject of the question.
made (someone) know / caused to realize you (single male)

A past-tense verb in Form IV (the a-…-ā shape), which makes the meaning causative: not merely "to know" but "to make someone know." The verb is 3rd-person masculine singular ("it/he made known"), its subject being the implied "what." Its closing weak letter is written as an undotted yā (ٮ) before the suffix —…
د-ر-ي (d-r-y) — to know, to be aware

Again the interrogative mā, "what," beginning the inner question "what is the Night of Qadr?" It is invariable and carries no case vowel. In the clause mā laylatu l-qadr, the standard parsing makes mā the subject (mubtadaʾ) and laylatu l-qadr the predicate (khabar);…

A feminine singular noun. The final -u ending is the nominative (subject) case, marking it as the topic of the question "what is the Night…" The word is in the construct (possessive) state with what follows, so its closing -t is pronounced, linking it to the next word: "the Night OF…"
the power / decree / measuring-out / worth (genitive ending)

Made of the definite article al- ("the") plus the noun qadr. The final -i ending is the genitive case: it is governed by the preceding word "Night," completing the possessive link "the Night OF Power/Decree." The article makes it a specific, known thing — the Decree, not just any decree.
ق-د-ر (q-d-r) — to measure out, ordain; power, worth

verses.ar: لَيۡلَةُ ٱلۡقَدۡرِ خَيۡرٞ مِّنۡ أَلۡفِ شَهۡرٖ
verses.en: The Night of Decree is better than a thousand months—sincere worship that night outweighs decades of ordinary time.
words: A feminine singular noun, and the first term of a construct (idafa) with the following 'al-Qadr', so the pair means 'Night of the Decree'. The final -u is nominative because the whole construct phrase is the subject of the sentence (the thing described as 'better').…

A masculine noun with the definite article 'al-' ('the'). The final -i is genitive, which is what makes the phrase mean 'Night OF the Decree', completing the construct (idafa) with 'laylatu' before it. The genitive is triggered because the noun is the second term of the construct ('Night of ___').
ق-د-ر (q-d-r) — to measure out, decree, determine; also power and worth
good / better (indefinite nominative ending, with tanwin)

An elative/comparative noun ('better, more excellent') serving as the predicate — the word that completes 'The Night... is ___'. The final -un is nominative with tanwin (the doubled-vowel 'n' sound), marking it as indefinite ('better', not 'the better') and agreeing in case with the subject it describes.…
خ-ي-ر (kh-y-r) — goodness, that which is good or preferable

A preposition. On its own 'min' usually means 'from', but after a comparative word like 'khayrun' ('better') it means 'than'. It governs the noun after it, forcing that noun into the genitive (which is why the next word, alfi, ends in -i); 'min' itself does not change form.…

A noun meaning 'thousand'. The final -i is genitive because it directly follows the preposition 'min' ('than'), which always puts the next word into the genitive. It is itself the first term of a construct with the following word, so it reads 'a thousand OF months'.
ا-ل-ف (ʾ-l-f) — a thousand; also the sense of joining/being numerous

A masculine singular noun. The final -in is genitive with tanwin: genitive because it is the second term of the construct after 'alf' ('a thousand of ___'), and the tanwin ('n' sound) marks it as indefinite.…
ش-ه-ر (sh-h-r) — month; the lunar month, linked to 'making visible' (the new moon)

verses.ar: تَنَزَّلُ ٱلۡمَلَٰٓئِكَةُ وَٱلرُّوحُ فِيهَا بِإِذۡنِ رَبِّهِم مِّن كُلِّ أَمۡرٖ
verses.en: On it the angels and the Spirit—Jibril—descend by their Lord's permission with every decreed matter—
words: they descend repeatedly / come down in waves

A present/future-tense verb in Form V (tafaʿʿala), here تَنَزَّلَ. Form V is built by prefixing ta- to the doubled-middle stem (nazzala), and that ta- is part of the verb pattern itself, not a personal pronoun-prefix; the doubled middle root letter gives the sense 'come down again and again, in abundance'.…

A definite plural noun: 'al-' means 'the'. It is the doer (subject) of the verb 'descend', which is why it carries the -u ending — the -u marks the nominative, the case used for the one performing the action. The noun is the broken plural of malak, 'angel'.
م-ل-ك (m-l-k) — message-bearing, sending; an angel is a bearer of Allah's command

'wa-' is the joining word 'and'. The noun 'the Spirit' is also a subject of 'descend' (alongside 'the angels'), so it too takes the -u nominative ending. Note the sun-letter rule: the 'l' of al- assimilates into the following 'r', so it is pronounced 'ar-rūḥu' (written الرُّوۡحُ with a shadda on the r).
ر-و-ح (r-w-ḥ) — spirit, breath of life, relief
in / during it (referring to the Night, feminine)

A preposition 'in/during' with an attached pronoun '-hā' meaning 'it'. The -hā is feminine because it refers back to 'the Night' (laylah), which is grammatically feminine in Arabic — hence 'in it' = 'in that night'. Attached pronouns after a preposition stay fixed in form and take no case ending.

The prefix 'bi-' means 'by/with' and shows the means. Because 'bi-' is a preposition, the noun after it takes the genitive -i ending: 'idhni' ('permission'). That same -i also links it to the next word, so it reads 'by the permission OF...' — 'idhni' is in a possessive chain with 'their Lord' that follows.
ا-ذ-ن (ʾ-dh-n) — granting permission, giving leave (also: ear, listening)
Lord, Master, Sustainer their (referring to the angels)

A noun 'Lord' with the attached pronoun '-him' ('their'), referring to the angels and the Spirit. The base word shows the genitive -i ('rabbi') because it completes the chain begun by 'bi-idhni' — literally 'by the permission OF the Lord OF them' = 'by their Lord's permission'.…

A preposition. Its basic sense is 'from', but in this verse it functions to mean 'with / on account of / concerning' every matter — it ties the descent to the decrees being brought down. As a preposition it has no ending of its own, but it forces the next word into the genitive.…

A quantifier meaning 'every/all'. It carries the genitive -i ending ('kulli') for two reasons: it follows the preposition 'min', and it is itself joined in a possessive chain to the next word — 'every OF matter' = 'every matter'. The -i is the link-and-after-preposition case.

A singular indefinite noun completing 'kulli' ('every matter'). The ending is -in (written with two kasras, tanwīn): the -i shows it is genitive (because it finishes the 'every OF...' chain), and the final -n ('tanwīn') marks it as indefinite — 'a matter / any matter' rather than 'the matter'.…
ا-م-ر (ʾ-m-r) — commanding, ordering; an affair or matter

verses.en: Peace it is until the breaking of dawn—mercy without turmoil until morning comes.
words: peace, safety, soundness a / some (indefinite nominative marker)

A singular indefinite noun. The final -un ending (called tanwīn, the 'n' sound on the end) marks it as indefinite — 'peace' in general, not 'the peace.' That -un is also the nominative case, which signals that this word is the predicate placed first for emphasis: 'Peace it is' — i.e., the whole night IS peace.
س-ل-م (s-l-m) — peace, safety, soundness, being free from fault or harm

A detached subject pronoun, third-person feminine singular ('it,' referring to the feminine noun 'night'). It is fixed in form and does not change for case. Coming after the predicate salāmun, the natural English order is 'It is peace,' though the Arabic fronts 'peace' for emphasis.

A particle of limit/end-point. Here it works like a preposition of 'until,' so the noun after it takes the genitive case (you can see this in the -i ending on the following word). It tells you when the night's peace reaches its boundary.
place/time of rising or emergence of (genitive case ending)

A noun of place/time built on the mīm- pattern (ma- prefix), meaning 'the point at which (something) rises.' Its final -i ending is genitive, caused by 'until' before it ('until the rising...'). It is also in a possessive link (iḍāfa) with the next word: 'the rising OF the dawn.'
ط-ل-ع (ṭ-l-ʿ) — to rise, come up, appear, emerge (as the sun or a star)
the dawn, daybreak of (genitive case ending)

The definite article al- ('the') plus the noun 'dawn.' The final -i ending is genitive because this word is the second part of a possessive pair (iḍāfa): 'the rising of the dawn.' So it specifies exactly what is rising — the dawn.
ف-ج-ر (f-j-r) — to break/split open; hence the dawn that 'splits' the darkness

surahs.name: الكَافِرُون
surahs.why: Six verses built almost entirely from one root (ع-ب-د, worship) and the negations لا وما — pure high-frequency grammar practice.

verses.en: Say to them: O you who reject what Allah revealed—this is not a negotiation of shared gods.
words: This is a command (imperative) verb addressed to a single male — "you, say!" The vowels have been stripped down to the bare command form (the underlying root vowel is dropped and the final letter carries a silent sukūn, shown by the small circle), which is the normal shape of a direct order in Arabic.…
ق-و-ل (q-w-l) — to say, to speak, to utter
O! (call to get attention) you/one (the word the call lands on) (particle of alerting; sharpens the call)

This is a vocative construction — the way you call out to someone. "yā" is the call-word "O"; "ayyu" is a noun that the call actually lands on and that introduces the named group to follow; "hā" is a particle of alerting (ḥarf tanbīh) tacked on to rouse the addressee's attention —…
the ones who reject faith / cover up the truth (plural ending: more than two males)

This is "the" plus an active participle — a doer-word meaning "the ones who disbelieve." The "-ūna" ending marks it as a sound masculine plural (three or more males) and is the nominative form ("-ūna" rather than "-īna").…
ك-ف-ر (k-f-r) — to cover, conceal; hence to reject or deny faith

verses.en: I do not worship what you worship—my devotion is not directed toward the powers, idols, or priorities you treat as ultimate.
words: This is the negating word 'not'. It comes directly before the verb that follows and cancels it, giving the sense 'I do not / I will not'. It has no ending of its own to parse — it is a fixed particle. Negating the present-tense verb after it conveys a settled, continuous refusal.

A present-tense verb. The a- prefix at the front marks the doer as 'I' (first person singular). The final -u ending is the indicative mood, meaning the action is stated as a plain, ongoing fact ('I worship'), not a command or wish. Coming after lā, it reads 'I do not worship'.
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, submit as a devoted servant

A relative word meaning 'what' or 'that which'. It is the object of the verb before it ('I worship [what]') and at the same time sets up the verb after it ('what you worship'). It is a fixed particle with no case ending to parse.
you (do) worship / serve (masculine plural) you all

A present-tense verb addressed to a group. The ta- prefix marks the doer as 'you', and the -ūna ending is a single, indivisible suffix that makes the subject masculine plural ('you all') while also marking the indicative mood, stating the action as a fact ('you worship').…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, submit as a devoted servant

verses.en: Nor do you worship what I worship—you have not surrendered to Allah alone as I have been commanded to do.
words: Two particles fused together. 'wa-' is the conjunction 'and', linking this verse to the one before. 'lā' is the negating particle 'not'; placed before a noun sentence here, it denies the statement as a whole. Neither word changes form for case or tense — particles are fixed.

A standalone subject pronoun, second person masculine plural. The '-tum' part is the marker for 'you all (men)'. As a pronoun it is indeclinable — it never takes case endings; it simply functions here as the subject of the sentence, with 'worshippers' as the predicate.
worshipper / one who worships (masculine plural ending)

An active participle, masculine plural. The '-ūna' ending marks it as plural, masculine, and in the nominative (subject) case — fitting because it is the predicate describing 'you' (antum). The participle form (shape ʿābid) names the doer of the act.…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself

A relative pronoun meaning 'what' or 'that which'. It heads a relative clause completed by the verb that follows (mā aʿbudu = 'that which I worship'). It is fixed in form — no case ending. The whole relative clause it introduces, not the word mā by itself, serves as the object of 'worshippers'.

A present-tense verb, first person singular. The 'a-' prefix means 'I' (the one doing the action). The final '-u' ending marks the indicative mood — a plain, ongoing statement of fact ('I worship', habitually and presently), not a command or a negated/subjunctive form. With 'mā' it forms 'what I worship'.
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself

verses.en: I will never worship what you worship—no social pressure, no compromise for peace at the price of shirk, will change that.
words: Two particles fused into one word. The prefix wa- means "and" and ties this verse to the one before it. lā is the negation "not"; here it negates the noun-based (nominal) sentence that follows ("I am not a worshipper of..."), giving an absolute, ongoing denial rather than a one-time "I did not."

An independent subject pronoun ("I"), not attached to any verb. It is indeclinable, so its ending never changes for case. Placed here as the subject (mubtadaʾ) of a nominal sentence ("I [am] a worshipper..."), its presence adds emphasis and contrast: "I, on my side."
worshipper / one who worships a (indefinite marker)

An active participle (the "-er" form) on the pattern fāʿil, meaning "a worshipper / one worshipping." The final -un is nunation carrying the -u (nominative) vowel: the -u marks it as the predicate (khabar) of the nominal sentence ("I [am] a worshipper"), and the -n shows it is indefinite ("a" worshipper, not "the").…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself
what / that which (here: the act of)

Indeclinable particle, so it has no case ending of its own. Two readings: (1) the dominant one for this verse, mā masdariyyah (infinitival) — it turns the following verb into a verbal noun, "your worshipping / your mode of worship," yielding "I will never adopt the way you worship";…
A perfect-tense verb with the attached subject ending -tum, "you," masculine plural — "you (all) worship / have worshipped." The doer is built into the suffix, so there is no separate prefix.…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, devote oneself

verses.en: Nor will you ever worship what I worship—until truth enters the heart, no argument can force genuine submission.
words: This is the prefix particle wa- ("and"), used to connect clauses. It carries no case ending of its own; it simply attaches to whatever follows and ties this line to the one before it. In the running verse it pairs with the negative that follows to give "and not / nor."

This is the negating particle lā. It is invariable — it never takes a case ending and never changes form. It works by denying the statement that follows; combined with the earlier wa- it yields "and not / nor."

This is a standalone subject pronoun, second person masculine plural. The piece ant- carries the "you," and the ending -um marks "plural / more than two males." Being an independent pronoun it has a fixed shape and takes no case vowel; placed first, it stresses the subject — "as for you..."
worshipper / one who worships (masculine plural ending)
This is an active participle, masculine plural. The stem ʿābid means "one who worships"; the ending -ūna marks masculine plural in the subject (nominative) case —…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, to serve, to devote oneself
that which / (my) manner of I worship (indicative-mood ending)

"Mā" is an invariable particle with no case ending; here it is read either as a relative pronoun ("that which") or, in the interpretation the source prefers for this verse, as mā maṣdariyya (an infinitival particle that recasts the following verb as a verbal noun, "my worship / way of worshipping").…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, to serve, to devote oneself

verses.en: For you is your deen—the way of life you have chosen. And for me is mine—the path of tawhid I will not abandon.
words: for / belonging to you (plural, men addressed)

A preposition plus an attached pronoun. The preposition is li- ("for/to"), but when the pronoun -kum attaches, its vowel shifts from -i to -a, giving la-kum. The -kum ending means "you all" (masculine plural), addressing the disbelievers. As a prepositional phrase placed first, it carries the sense "to you belongs...".
religion / way of life / requital (subject-marking ending) your (plural, men addressed)

A noun with a possessive ending. The -u vowel on dīn-u is the nominative case: it marks this word as the subject of the verbless sentence ("your religion [is] for you"). The attached -kum means "your" (masculine plural), so together: "your religion."
د-ي-ن (d-y-n) — religion, way of life, judgement/requital

Three pieces joined: the connector wa- ("and"), the preposition li- ("for"), and the pronoun for "me." When the "me" pronoun attaches to li-, it surfaces with an -a vowel here (li-ya), giving wa-li-ya. The phrase means "and to me belongs...".
religion / way of life / requital (remnant of "my")
The same noun dīn, here ending in -i. In this Qur'anic reading the possessive "my" (the yāʾ) is dropped at the end of the verse, and the leftover kasra (-i) stands in its place —…
د-ي-ن (d-y-n) — religion, way of life, judgement/requital

surahs.name: النَّصْر
surahs.why: Three verses with إذا، جاء، رأيت، كان — four of the highest-frequency words in the Quran, in their natural habitat.

verses.en: When Allah's decisive help arrives and victory becomes visible—not the small wins of ego, but the opening you could not manufacture yourself—
words: An indeclinable particle, so it has no case ending that changes. It introduces a future event treated as certain to occur and signals that a 'main clause' (the response) is still coming later in the passage.

A past-tense verb, third-person masculine singular ('he/it came'), matching its subject 'help' which follows. It carries no added person/gender suffix — the bare third-masculine-singular form; the fatḥa on the final letter (the hamza) is part of the verb's own shape, not a separate tense or person marker.…

A masculine noun functioning as the subject of 'came'. The final -u ending (nominative) marks it as the doer of the verb — it is the help that 'comes'. It is also the first part of a possessive pair, joined to 'Allah' next ('the help OF Allah').
Allah (the divine name) of — possessive ending

The divine name in the genitive (possessive) case, shown by the final -i ending, which links it to the preceding word: 'the help OF Allah'. The name اللّٰه is treated by Arabic grammarians as a single, undivided proper noun;…
and the victory / opening, conquest (subject marker)

'wa-' is 'and'; 'al-' is 'the'. The noun is a second subject of 'came', sharing the verb with 'help'. Its final -u ending (nominative) marks it as part of what 'comes'. The leading 'al-' makes it definite — THE victory, the well-known promised one.

verses.ar: وَرَأَيۡتَ ٱلنَّاسَ يَدۡخُلُونَ فِي دِينِ ٱللَّهِ أَفۡوَاجٗا
verses.en: and you see people entering Allah's deen in waves, no longer mocking what they once dismissed,
words: A past-tense verb. The wa- at the front simply means "and," joining this to the previous verse. The core verb ra'ā means "to see." The final -ta is the subject ending that means "you" (one man) did the action — here, the Prophet himself. So the whole is "and you saw / witnessed."
ر-أ-ي (r-ʾ-y) — to see, perceive, behold (the middle radical is the hamza, ء)

A noun with "the" attached at the front (the l of al- assimilates into the n, so it sounds like an-nās). The final -a is the accusative ending: it marks this word as the direct object — the thing being seen. So it answers "you saw whom? — the people."
أ-ن-س (ʾ-n-s) — humans, sociability; nās comes from unās, with the initial hamza dropped
they (he-type, marks present tense) enter, go in they (masculine plural)

A present/ongoing-tense verb. The ya- prefix signals the present tense and a third-person subject, and the -ūna ending makes it "they" (many men) and keeps the verb in its normal (indicative) state, i.e. simple ongoing action: "they are entering." It describes the people of the previous word.

A preposition (a small linking word) meaning "in" or "into." It has no endings that change; it governs the noun after it, putting that noun into the genitive case. Here it links "entering" to its destination, "the religion of Allah."
— (a particle; it has no triliteral root)
religion, way of life, obedience to Allah

A noun. The final -i ending is genitive for two reasons working together: it follows the preposition fī ("in"), and it links to the next word to mean "the religion OF…" So it reads "into the religion of [Allah]."
The proper name Allah. The final -i ending is genitive, showing possession after the preceding word: "the religion OF Allah." It links the religion to its owner.
— (the proper name of Allah; not derived from a triliteral root in the ordinary way)
in crowds, in throngs, in troops (groups of people)

A single plural noun in the accusative indefinite (its ending carries tanwīn al-fatḥ, the "-an" you hear). It is not the direct object here; it works as a ḥāl — a word describing the state or manner in which the people entered: "crowd after crowd, in throngs." So: they were entering the religion of Allah in droves.
ف-و-ج (f-w-j) — a group, troop, or band of people

verses.ar: فَسَبِّحۡ بِحَمۡدِ رَبِّكَ وَٱسۡتَغۡفِرۡهُۚ إِنَّهُۥ كَانَ تَوَّابَۢا
verses.en: then glorify your Lord with deep praise and seek His forgiveness—for even on the day of triumph, a servant remains in need of mercy, and He is ever the One who accepts tawbah.
words: so / then (in response to what came before) glorify! / declare (Him) free of all imperfection

"fa-" is a connecting prefix meaning "so/then," linking this response to the conditions in verses 1-2. "sabbiḥ" is a form II (doubled-middle-letter) command verb, which makes the action intensive and deliberate: to actively and repeatedly declare Allah's perfection.…
س-ب-ح (s-b-ḥ) — to swim/glide away; hence to declare Allah utterly removed and free from all imperfection (glorification, tasbīḥ)
with / by means of the praise (of)

"bi-" is an attached preposition meaning "with/by." Any noun after it must take the genitive case, which is why "ḥamd" appears with a final "-i" (ḥamdi). That same "-i" ending also chains it to the next word, giving "praise OF (your Lord)."
ح-م-د (ḥ-m-d) — praise; commending someone for their excellence and goodness
Lord / Master and Sustainer your (one male)

"rabb" is the first term of a possessive construct ("the Lord OF you" = "your Lord"), and it joins directly to the possessor "-ka" without any separate word for "of." Its final "-i" (rabbi) is the genitive case carried over from the preposition "bi-" two words back, which governs this whole "praise-of-your-Lord" phrase…
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, master, sustain; one who owns and brings up
and seek forgiveness! / ask for pardon Him

"wa-" means "and." The verb is form X, marked by the "ist-" prefix, which carries the sense of "to seek or ask for" something — so from the root "to forgive" it becomes "to seek forgiveness." It is a command to a single male ("you"), ending in the jussive (no final vowel).…
غ-ف-ر (gh-f-r) — to cover, conceal, pardon; hence forgiveness

"inna" is a particle of emphasis and affirmation placed at the start of a statement to assert it firmly — best rendered "indeed" or "truly." It grammatically takes a following noun/pronoun in the accusative; here that is the attached pronoun "-hu" ("He"), referring to Allah. Together: "Indeed He..."

"kāna" is the past-tense verb "to be," third-person singular masculine ("He was/has been"). The final "-a" is its normal past-tense ending. Applied to Allah it expresses permanence — an unchanging attribute — so it is best read as "He has ever been" / "He always is."
ك-و-ن (k-w-n) — to be, to exist, to come to be
ever-accepting of repentance / oft-relenting (accusative ending, completing "He has ever been ___")

This is an intensive noun-pattern (faʿʿāl), which signals something done abundantly and repeatedly — hence "ever / oft" accepting repentance, not merely "accepting" once. The final "-an" (tanwīn) is the accusative ending because it is the complement of "kāna" ("He has ever BEEN ever-relenting");…
ت-و-ب (t-w-b) — to turn back, return; repentance (from the servant) and acceptance of it / relenting (from Allah)

surahs.name: المَسَد
surahs.why: Short narrative verses with past-tense verbs (تبّ، أغنى، كسب) — a story you already know, told in five lines.

verses.en: Ruin seize the hands of Abu Lahab—and may he himself perish—he who mocked the Message publicly and thought his tribe could protect him from consequences.
words: to perish, be ruined, come to nothing feminine-singular subject ending (agreeing with 'hands')

A past-tense verb (form I) used as a curse/invocation: 'may it perish!' The ending -at (ـَتْ) marks the subject as third-person feminine singular. It is feminine singular — not plural — because the grammatical subject is yadā ('the two hands');…
ت-ب-ب (t-b-b) — to perish, be ruined, suffer loss

This is the dual form of yad ('hand'), so it means exactly 'two hands,' not one or many. It is the subject of the verb 'perish.' The dual normally ends in -āni, but because it is joined ('in construct') to the next word 'Abu (Lahab)' — 'the two hands OF Abu Lahab' — the final -ni drops, leaving just yadā.

This is one of a small set of 'five nouns' that show their case with a long vowel. Here the -ī ending is the genitive (possessive) form, signalling two links at once: 'the hands OF Abu…' (it is possessed by 'hands') and 'Abu OF Lahab' (it owns the next word). So abī = 'father of,' set up to attach to 'Lahab.'
flame, blaze the '-in' ending: genitive + indefinite (no 'the')

The final -in (written with the double-vowel mark called tanwīn) does two things: the -i part is genitive because this word is possessed by 'Abu' ('father OF Lahab'), and the -n part marks it as indefinite (no 'the'). Together they give the reading 'Father of Flame.'

'wa' (وَ) means 'and.' 'tabba' is the same form I past-tense verb as in word 1, but here it is third-person masculine singular, so the subject is Abu Lahab himself rather than 'the hands.' Masculine singular is shown by the absence of any subject suffix —…
ت-ب-ب (t-b-b) — to perish, be ruined, suffer loss

verses.ar: مَآ أَغۡنَىٰ عَنۡهُ مَالُهُۥ وَمَا كَسَبَ
verses.en: His wealth and whatever he accumulated will not rescue him when the account is due—no inheritance, no status, no emergency fund can pay that debt.
words: A standalone negative particle, here negating the past-tense verb that follows. It has no case ending of its own; it simply turns 'availed' into 'did NOT avail.' The lengthening on the alif is a recitation feature, not a grammatical ending.
availed / warded off (Form IV) he/it (3rd-person masc. past ending)

A past-tense verb, 3rd-person masculine singular ('it availed'). It is Form IV (formed with a prefixed hamza, اَ-, on the root), which makes the verb causative/transitive: 'to make someone free of need,' i.e.…
غ-ن-ي (gh-n-y) — to be free of need, richness, sufficiency
from / on behalf of him (attached pronoun)

A preposition ('ʿan') with an attached pronoun ('-hu' = him). The pronoun is in the object form required after a preposition. Together they tell us the wealth did not avail 'for/from him' — pinning the benefit that never came to Abu Lahab specifically.

A noun ('māl' = wealth) plus the attached possessive pronoun '-hu' (his). The -u ending on 'mālu' is the nominative case, marking this as the doer/subject of the verb 'availed' — it is 'his wealth' that did the (non-)availing.

Two particles: 'wa' (and) joining this to 'his wealth,' and 'mā' here used not as a negative but as a relative pronoun meaning 'what / that which.' So the second subject of 'availed' is 'and that which he earned.' These particles take no case ending.

A past-tense verb, 3rd-person masculine singular: 'he earned.' The final -a is the standard past-tense ending for 'he (did).' It sits inside the relative clause begun by 'mā,' giving 'that which he earned.' Together with 'his wealth,' it forms the compound subject of 'availed him nothing.'

verses.en: He will enter a Fire of blazing flame—
words: shall / will soon (future marker) he (3rd-person masculine prefix) roast / be burned by fire

A present/future verb made future by the prefix sa- ('will, shall soon'). The ya- prefix marks a single male subject: 'he'. The verb ends in a long -ā (written with alif maqṣūra) because its final root letter is weak; it carries the indicative meaning of a real, certain event. Sense: 'he will surely be burned/roasted.'
ص-ل-ي (ṣ-l-y) — to roast, burn, be scorched by fire

A noun in the accusative case, shown by the final -an (tanwīn fatḥa). The accusative marks it as the object/goal of the verb — what he will be burned in. The -an ending also signals that the noun is indefinite: 'a fire.'
ن-و-ر (n-w-r) — the sense of burning/blazing fire; the noun نَار ('fire') belongs to this root (distinct from نُور, 'light,' from the same root)

The feminine form of dhū ('possessor of'). It is in the accusative case, shown by the final -a, because it describes the accusative noun 'fire' (nār is feminine, so the feminine dhāt agrees with it). It is grammatically bound to the word after it, meaning 'having ___ / possessor of ___.'
— (a possessive word; no verbal triliteral root)

A noun in the genitive case, shown by the final -in (tanwīn kasra). It is genitive because it follows dhāta ('possessor of'), forming a possessive link (iḍāfa): 'possessor OF flame' = 'full of flame.' The -in ending also marks it as indefinite.
ل-ه-ب (l-h-b) — to blaze, flame up; flame

verses.en: and his wife too, the carrier of thorns—she who spread harm alongside him, fueling hostility against the truth.
words: Starts with the connecting word "and" (wa-), joining this verse to what precedes.…
م-ر-ء (m-r-ʾ) — person / human being; feminine form: woman, wife
This is an intensive doer-form (the faʿʿaal pattern, with the middle letter doubled) made feminine by the ـة ending — "one who carries a great deal." The doubling stresses repetition and intensity.…
ح-م-ل (ḥ-m-l) — to carry, bear, haul a load

Begins with the definite article al- ("the"). The stem "ḥaṭab" (firewood) carries the ending -i (genitive): the -i marks it as the thing possessed by "carrier," giving the meaning "carrier OF the firewood" — the genitive link is exactly what English shows with "of."
ح-ط-ب (ḥ-ṭ-b) — firewood, to gather wood for fuel

verses.en: Around her neck will be a rope of palm fibre—a symbol of the burden she chose and the humiliation that follows arrogance against Allah.
words: An indeclinable preposition (a fixed little word that never changes its form). Its job is to point to a location: "in/on her neck." Whatever noun comes after it must take the genitive ending — which is exactly why the next word reads jīdi-hā (with an -i) rather than jīdu-hā.

A noun followed by an attached possessive pronoun. The -i ending on jīdi is genitive, caused by the preposition fī before it ("in her neck"). The suffix -hā means "her," referring to a single female; because this pronoun is possessing the noun, the two together form an "of" relationship — literally "the neck of her."

A singular noun carrying the ending -un, a form of tanwīn (nunation) written above the final letter as two small ḍamma strokes (tiny curly hooks), not as two dots. That doubled-vowel ending marks the word as indefinite —…

An indeclinable preposition (a fixed word that does not change form). It governs the following noun and forces it into the genitive case — which is why the next word ends in -in. In this context it conveys "made of / consisting of," describing the rope's material.

A singular noun with the ending -in, a form of tanwīn (nunation) written beneath the final letter as two small kasra strokes. The -i part is the genitive case, required because the preposition min comes before it ("of palm-fibre"). The tanwīn (the doubled vowel) marks it as indefinite —…
م-س-د (m-s-d) — to twist / tightly wind (a fibre rope)

surahs.name: قُرَيْش
surahs.why: Paired with Al-Fil in recitation — worship the Lord of this House, who feeds against hunger and secures against fear. You will stand at 'this House'.

verses.en: For the accustomed security of Quraysh—
words: for / because of (a preposition giving the reason or purpose) making accustomed, habituation, granting settled familiarity (the final '-i' here is the genitive case vowel required by the preposition 'li', not a separate word-part)

The 'li' prefix is a preposition meaning 'for' or 'because of'; it both gives a reason (in amazement at, or in gratitude for, Allah's favor) and, on another reading, points forward to the command in ayah 3 ('let them worship...').…
ء-ل-ف (ʾ-l-f) — to be familiar, intimate, accustomed; to join/unite in harmony
Quraysh, the tribe of Makkah (the final '-in' is the genitive case ending with nunation, marking it as the possessor of the preceding 'īlāf', not a separate word-part)

This is a proper name (the tribe Quraysh) and the second term of a possessive construction (iḍāfah): 'the familiarity/habituation OF Quraysh', so it stands in the genitive case. In the Quranic text it is read 'qurayshin', with the genitive marked by nunation (tanwīn, the final 'n').…
ق-ر-ش (q-r-sh) — to gather, collect, earn; 'Quraysh' is commonly explained as a diminutive form from this root (and is also linked to qirsh, a sea creature/shark)

verses.en: Their accustomed trading journeys in winter to Yemen and summer to Syria—safe passage others did not enjoy—
words: making accustomed / habitual familiarity (the -i ending: 'of', genitive linking vowel) their (them)
This is a verbal noun (maṣdar) from form IV (the 'a-/ī- causative' pattern), so it carries the sense of 'causing to become familiar / making habituated', not merely 'being familiar'.…
أ-ل-ف (ʾ-l-f) — to be familiar with, to grow accustomed, to join together in intimacy
a journey / caravan trip (feminine noun stem; the ة is pronounced -t- here) (the -a ending on the ة: object marker)

The noun stem رِحۡلَة ends in a tāʾ marbūṭa (ة), the feminine marker that is part of the word itself; it marks a single instance of the act ('one journey'). In connected speech and in this possessive construction the ة is sounded as -t-, and the case vowel sits on it, so the word reads riḥla-t-a.…
ر-ح-ل (r-ḥ-l) — to depart, set out, journey; a mount/saddle for travel

The prefix al- is 'the' (the 'l' is silent here and doubles the following 'sh' — pronounced ash-shitāʾ). The final vowel is -i (l-shitāi), the genitive case, because it is possessed by the previous word: 'the journey OF the winter'. So this word links back to riḥla to mean 'the winter journey'.

The prefix wa- means 'and', joining this to 'the winter'. The prefix al- is 'the' (the 'l' is silent and doubles the 'ṣ' — pronounced aṣ-ṣayf). The final vowel is -i (wal-ṣayfi), the genitive case, matching 'the winter': it too is possessed by 'the journey', giving 'the journey of winter AND summer'.

verses.en: Let them worship the Rabb of this House—the Ka'bah through which their safety and livelihood flowed—
words: so / then (a connector drawing a conclusion) let / must (command particle) they worship (silent spelling alif after the plural -ū)

The fa- links this back to the earlier favours ('so, in response...'). The la- is the 'command' particle (lam al-amr) that turns a statement into an order aimed at a third party ('let them...'). Following the connector fa-, this lam takes no vowel of its own (sukun) and is read straight on as fal-yaʿbudū —…
ع-ب-د (ʿ-b-d) — to worship, serve, and submit in devotion

This is the object of 'worship' — the One they are commanded to worship. The final -a ending is the accusative case, which marks it as the direct object ('worship the Lord'). It is also the first term of a possessive construction (idafa) with the following words, so it reads 'the Lord OF this House.'
ر-ب-ب (r-b-b) — to nurture, sustain, master, and bring to completion

A demonstrative ('this') referring to a single masculine thing that is near. It is indeclinable, so it keeps the same form regardless of case; it simply points to 'the House' that follows and ties the phrase together as 'this House.'
The al- prefix is 'the,' marking it as a specific, well-known house (the Ka'bah). The -i ending is the genitive case.…
ب-ي-ت (b-y-t) — to spend the night, dwell; hence bayt, a house or dwelling

verses.ar: ٱلَّذِيٓ أَطۡعَمَهُم مِّن جُوعٖ وَءَامَنَهُم مِّنۡ خَوۡفِۭ
verses.en: Who fed them against hunger and secured them against fear.
words: A masculine singular relative pronoun ('the one who'). It is built (indeclinable) so its ending never changes for case. Here it functions as a description of Allah, linking back to 'the Lord of this House' and setting up the verbs that come next.

A past-tense (form IV, causative) verb: the a- prefix with this pattern turns 'to eat' into 'to make eat / to feed'. The verb is third-person masculine singular ('He'), referring to Allah; the final -a is the normal past-tense ending. The attached -hum ('them') is the object — the people being fed.

A preposition ('from'). It is built (indeclinable) and does not change. It governs the noun that follows ('hunger'), putting it in the genitive case; in context it expresses removal from or protection against that condition.
An indefinite noun ('hunger'). The final -in ending (tanwīn, the 'n' sound) marks it as indefinite ('hunger / a state of hunger') and as genitive because it follows the preposition 'from'.
and he made safe / gave security them

The wa- prefix means 'and', joining this to the previous verb. The verb itself is past tense, form IV (causative): 'to make safe / to give security' (built from the root meaning 'to be safe'). Form IV normally takes a prefixed hamza on the pattern afʿala, which here would give aʾmana (a- prefix + root-initial hamza).…

A preposition ('from'). It is built (indeclinable) and does not change. It governs the following noun ('fear'), putting it in the genitive case; here it conveys protection or safety from that condition.
An indefinite noun ('fear'). The final -in ending (tanwīn, the 'n' sound) marks it as indefinite ('fear / a state of fear') and as genitive because it follows the preposition 'from'.

surahs.name: الفِيل
surahs.why: The army that came for the Kaaba — five verses of vivid past-tense narrative (أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ).

verses.ar: أَلَمۡ تَرَ كَيۡفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصۡحَٰبِ ٱلۡفِيلِ
verses.en: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the army of the elephant—when worldly power marched against His House, confident that sheer force decides history?
words: Two particles joined together. The a- at the front turns the statement into a question. lam is a negative particle that puts the verb after it into the jussive mood and pushes its meaning into the past, so 'lam tara' = 'you did not see / have not seen'.…
'you' (the doer; masculine singular) the verb stem 'see'; its middle root-letter hamza (ء) and its final weak root-letter ( y) have both dropped out on the surface, leaving just 'ra'

A present-tense verb governed by the preceding 'lam', which puts it in the jussive mood and shifts its time-frame to the past: 'you have (not) seen'. The ta- prefix marks the subject as 'you' (one male person).…

An indeclinable question-word (it never changes its form for case). It fixes permanently on a final '-a' (fatḥah) ending. It introduces and is the object of the verb that follows ('how He dealt'), framing the entire clause as a question about manner.

A plain past-tense verb in its base form (form I). With no attached subject pronoun, the bare stem with its '-a' ending built in means 'he did' — a single male doer, here 'your Lord'. The final '-a' is simply the standard vowel of the past-tense verb. The subject ('your Lord') comes right after as a separate word.
